item.title: What If
item.description: TODO: refer to standard notes disclaimer. Chapter 1 Q: why does the atmosphere of venus rotate at a high speed even though the underlying planet is almost stationary? A: this phenomenon is called super-rotation.

item.title: CS 183EW: Engineering Ethics
item.description: Taken Winter 2021, with Prof. Gershon Weltman. Nicely typeset PDF available here.

item.title: CS 132: Compiler Construction
item.description: Taken Fall 2021, with Prof. Jens Palsberg. What a great class. Nicely typeset PDF available here.

item.title: Evolution in the Deep Sea
item.description: My first non-technical and non-historical topic! Today is meant as a change of pace before we go on hiatus for the quarter; it’s information-light and full of pretty pictures of ocean life.

item.title: Real Git
item.description: This presentation is an intermediate-level guide to Git. It also serves as a primer to good version control conventions. If you’ve found yourself wondering why you have spurious “merged branch X into X” commits after a git pull, this presentation is for you.

item.title: CS M151B: Computer Architecture
item.description: Taken Winter 2021, with Prof. Glenn Reinman. Great class. Nicely typeset PDF available here.

item.title: The Two Webs
item.description: In this presentation, I talk about a shift in the nature of the World Wide Web’s content delivery systems – a shift from “automatically downloading documents via standard formats” to “portably running applications”.

item.title: CS 131: Programming Languages
item.description: Taken Winter 2020, with Prof. Paul Eggert. Nicely typeset PDF available here.

item.title: CS 143: Intro to Databases
item.description: Taken Spring 2021, with Prof. John Cho. PDF available here.

item.title: Spanner: Google vs the CAP Theorem
item.description: In this presentation, I explain how Google’s Spanner system is implemented – and how it’s performance seemingly defies the CAP theorem. I’ve tried to keep this presentation accessible to people without a deep systems background, but do let me know if you weren’t at my prior presentation about distributed systems.

item.title: Computer Terminal Protocols
item.description: Why do terminal emulators still mess up their output when you resize the window? In this presentation, I walk through the history of UNIX terminals. I discuss: Antique methods of electrical communication, from Morse code to the famous barbed wire telephone network used by American farmers.

item.title: Defining Time in Distributed Systems
item.description: In this presentation, I talk about: Race conditions in single-machine programming (TOCTOU, etc). How solving these race conditions necessitates atomic primitives. “Race conditions” in distributed systems (oh no! no more atomics!

item.title: Containers
item.description: What’s the difference between a containers and a virtual machine? How is a hypervisor/kernel actually implemented? Do these abstractions add runtime overhead? If so, how much, and where? This presentation attempts to provide clear, evidence based, answers to the above questions.

item.title: Ling 1: Intro to Linguistics
item.description: Taken Winter 2021. Fully asynchronous class. PDF available here.

item.title: CS 111: Operating Systems
item.description: Taken Fall 2019, with Prof. Paul Eggert. Nicely typeset PDF available here.

item.title: Hist 3A: History of Science
item.description: Taken Fall 2019, with Prof. Amir Alexander. Nicely typeset PDF available here.
